text: The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon controlling the uterine arteries at their origin. The assistant retracts the infundibulopelvic ligament medially, while the surgeon opens the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament, expanding the retroperitoneal space. The ureter is identified, and dissection proceeds above it into the pararectal space, revealing the uterine artery. The uterine artery is clipped at its origin, with the uterus visible below the forceps.

The surgeon performs the procedure on the opposite side, applying medial traction to the infundibulopelvic ligament. The assistant uses scissors to cut connective tissue in the retroperitoneal space after short coagulation. The uterus is visible, with the uterine artery located just above and in front of it. The surgeon carefully dissects the connective tissue to expose the uterine artery and vein, ensuring a bloodless laparoscopic myomectomy due to severe anemia.

The uterus is positioned under the scissors, with the uterine artery clearly visible. The surgeon coagulates the small connective tissue above the uterine artery and applies a clip for vascular control. The umbilical artery is seen lateral to the uterine artery. The surgeon begins the myomectomy with a longitudinal hystereotomy using bipolar current, making a six-centimeter incision for a nine-centimeter myoma. The dissection of the healthy myometrium is bloodless, and the surgeon attempts to locate and extract the myoma.

The surgeon performs a smooth dissection of a soft myoma, applying bipolar current directly to the myoma while avoiding the healthy myometrium. The myoma is close to the endometrium, requiring careful manipulation to prevent damage to the endometrial mucosa. Traction and contraction techniques are used to facilitate the myomectomy, with hydro-dissection as an optional aid. The uterine cavity is opened, and the myoma is removed without using energy near the endometrium to minimize the risk of synesia. Hemostasis is achieved through stitches rather than bipolar energy.

The surgeon uses a 14mm needle with Vicryl suture to place stitches 2-3 centimeters lateral to the edge of the hystereotomy. Intracorporeal knotting is performed to ensure precise control of the suture tension. The surgical field shows good ischemia with no bleeding, and three to four stitches are used for hemostasis. The healthy myometrium is invaginated into the myoma bed, reducing the risk of abdominal adhesions.

The surgeon sutures the lower part of the hystereotomy using a Vicryl one needle, ensuring the needle is positioned on the opposite side from the previous stitch. The technique involves locking the knot securely with two or three knots to achieve effective hemostasis of the myoma bed.

The surgeon places an additional stitch laterally on the upper part of the uterus for improved hemostasis, ensuring the tube is not included in the stitch. The knot is locked using an interrupted suture technique with Vicryl, providing a secure closure. The final suture aspect is shown, demonstrating effective hemostasis and minimizing the risk of intramyometrial hematoma and adhesions.

The surgeon places an endoscopic bag inside the abdomen and inserts the myoma into the bag for in-bag morcellation, aiming to reduce the risk of leomyomatosis or parasitic myoma. After complete morcellation, the bag is removed through a twelve millimeter trocar, followed by thorough cleaning of the abdominal and pelvic cavity. The uterine arteries are visually inspected, confirming no abnormal bleeding, with the clip remaining securely in place on the left uterine artery.
